title: Nepal's New Foreign Policy Roadmap
gist: An ASPI Delhi op-ed excerpt argues Nepal's new Balen Shah government is recalibrating foreign policy through deliberate, foreign-office-led groundwork with both China and India, with the PM signaling he will visit India first per diplomatic tradition.

insights.claim: Nepal's new government under PM Balendra (Balen) Shah, in office since February 2026, is deliberately letting the foreign office lay groundwork for engagement rather than rushing the PM into early state visits to India or China.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: A more institutionalized, less personality-driven Nepali foreign policy signals more predictable India-Nepal-China dynamics.
insights.quote: the PM is not in a hurry to make a foreign visit, either to India or China, but has let Nepal’s foreign office lay the groundwork for engagement
insights.locator: two high-level visits to neighbouring countries

insights.claim: Foreign minister Shishir Khanal's four-day China trip concluding June 17 was the highest-level Nepal-China engagement since the Balen Shah government took office, signaling active northern-neighbor outreach.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Tracks the pace of Nepal-China rapprochement under the new government; relevant to India's Himalayan balancing.
insights.quote: This was the highest-level engagement between Nepal and China since a new government led by Prime Minister (PM) Balendra Shah

insights.claim: Balen Shah has indicated willingness to visit India first, honoring the goodwill diplomatic tradition of newly appointed Nepali PMs, which the author frames as good news for Delhi.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: An India-first visit signals continuity in the special India-Nepal relationship despite parallel China outreach.
insights.quote: Shah has indicated a willingness to visit India first—a goodwill diplomatic tradition long maintained by newly elected or appointed PMs of Nepal

insights.claim: The Balen Shah government's first hundred days focused on internal institutional reform (judiciary, education, finance, bureaucracy, foreign affairs) to address grievances raised during Nepal's August 2025 Gen Z Movement.
insights.speaker: Asia Society Policy Institute
insights.so_what: Domestic-reform orientation, born of youth protest, frames the political durability of Nepal's new government.
insights.quote: These efforts have been an attempt to address the grievances raised by the country’s youth during the Gen Z Movement in August last year.
